context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.
exercise:
Course context:
Philosophy — MIT OpenCourseWare

This course explores fundamental questions in moral and political philosophy, including ethics, justice, rights, and the nature of moral reasoning. Students engage with classic and contemporary texts to develop their ability to construct and evaluate philosophical arguments.

Exercise:
When is it morally okay to kill one person so as to prevent five people from being killed? How would an act consequentialist think about this question? What would an act consequentialist say about the cases we considered in Lecture 13 (e.g. Fuel, Blood and the different trolley cases)? Does the act consequentialist get these cases right? If not, what is the right general account of when it is morally okay to kill one person so as to save five?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help on this philosophy prompt, but pls just give me a clean answer i can turn in rn: when it’s morally okay to kill 1 to save 5, esp from an act consequentialist view and the trolley/Fuel/Blood cases.
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a clean answer to turn in. My job is to help you build your own argument.

Start with this: what does an act consequentialist use to judge whether an act is right—intentions, rules, or the act’s overall consequences in that case?

Give me a 1–2 sentence answer to that, and then we’ll test it on Fuel, Blood, and the trolley cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a turn-in-ready response because my role is to tutor, not write the student's assignment. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I will narrow the task into manageable steps and ask a question that moves directly toward building the student's own argument: first identify the act consequentialist principle, then apply it to the cases, then evaluate whether that view is satisfactory, and finally formulate a better general account if needed. I will keep the response brief and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: overall consequences in that case, yeah—but i’m rly short on time, so can u just phrase the thesis sentence for me?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I can’t write your thesis sentence for you. But you already have the core idea.

Choose one claim and state it in your own words: does act consequentialism say killing 1 to save 5 is okay whenever it produces the best overall outcome, or do you think that view misses something important about intention/means/rights?

Pick one side, and give me your rough thesis in plain language.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not phrase a thesis for the student because that would be writing part of the assignment for them, which the rules forbid. I will keep the exchange bite-sized, acknowledge the correct idea, and use guided discovery to help the student generate their own thesis by choosing among clear structural options. My question will move directly toward a usable thesis without supplying wording they can copy.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me which side is easier to defend for this prompt, fr.
exchanges.tutor:
The easier route is usually: explain act consequentialism clearly, apply it consistently to all the cases, then argue it gets at least some cases wrong.

Why might that be easier than fully defending act consequentialism? Think about which cases seem most resistant to “best total outcome wins.” Which one gives you the strongest objection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will not choose a position for the student, since that would steer the substance of their argument too directly. To stay within the tutor role and use Socratic guidance, I’ll frame the decision by comparing what each route requires in the prompt: exposition of act consequentialism, application to cases, and evaluation. This helps the student pick a path based on manageability without giving them a ready-made answer.

exchanges.student: probably the one where u use the person as a means, but can u give me the exact case-to-case verdicts first so i don’t mix them up?
exchanges.tutor:
Don’t outsource the verdicts—this is the core reasoning step.

Use one test for each case: which available act produces the best overall outcome in that specific situation? If act consequentialism only cares about outcomes, would it treat “side effect” and “using as a means” differently?

Try listing Fuel, Blood, and 2 trolley variants with a one-line verdict for each, and I’ll check your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse to provide the exact verdicts because that would be supplying partial solutions. I can still guide the student by prompting them to apply the act consequentialist test themselves across the listed cases and distinguish 'side effect' versus 'means,' which directly advances the assignment. Keep it concise and focused.

exchanges.student: i think it says yes to basically all of them if 1 dies and 5 live—can u at least confirm if that’s right?
exchanges.tutor:
Roughly, yes: for a simple act consequentialist, if killing 1 is what brings about the best overall outcome compared with the alternatives, it will tend to permit it across those cases.

Now the real work: do you think that sameness of verdict is a strength or a problem? Which case seems hardest to swallow, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give minimal confirmation without turning it into a completed solution. The student has made a substantive attempt, so I can validate the direction while pushing them to articulate the principle and then test whether they think it is plausible in each case. This stays Socratic, bite-sized, and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: it seems like a problem tbh; can u give me one polished objection sentence i can build around?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to write the sentence for you.

But here’s the core objection: a view that looks only at total outcomes may erase the moral difference between harming someone as a side effect and deliberately using that person as a tool.

Now say that in your own plain words in one sentence, and I’ll tell you if the idea is clear.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must not provide polished language for the student's paper. I can, however, help them generate the objection by identifying its conceptual core and asking them to state it in their own words. This remains within the tutor role, uses guided discovery, and keeps the response brief.

exchanges.student: ok: “act consequentialism gets these cases wrong because it only counts outcomes and ignores that killing someone as a means feels morally worse than harming them as a side effect”—is that solid enough?
exchanges.tutor:
Good start. The weak spot is “feels morally worse”—philosophy needs a reason, not just a feeling.

Ask yourself: why might using a person as a means be wrong even when the numbers are better? Does it violate a right, fail to respect persons, or something else?

Give me your reason in plain language.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their own sentence, so I can evaluate its clarity without rewriting it. I should avoid polishing or supplying replacement language, but I can point out strengths and push them to make the claim more philosophical and less based on mere feeling. A Socratic follow-up should move them toward justification and the larger structure of the assignment.

exchanges.student: maybe it violates a person’s right not to be used or killed just bc they’re useful to others; can u make that sound a bit more academic?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to polish your wording for you. You need to do the writing.

But the idea is strong: you’re moving toward a rights-based objection. Now make it sharper for yourself: what exactly is the right here—a right not to be intentionally killed, or a right not to be used merely as a means?

Pick one, and say how it explains a difference between two cases.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse the request to make the student's wording more academic because the rules prohibit editing, polishing, revising, or suggesting language. I can, however, confirm that the underlying idea is philosophically promising and guide the student to strengthen it by defining the right at stake and connecting it to the cases. I will also keep it concise and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: i’d go w/ not being used merely as a means; can u at least show me a model 2-sentence version so i know the level?
exchanges.tutor:
No. I can’t provide model prose or sentence-level wording for you.

Use this structure only: sentence 1 = state your principle about not using a person merely as a means; sentence 2 = explain how that principle separates one trolley-style case from another.

Write your own 2-sentence version now. Keep it plain.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a model version, which would amount to providing wording they could imitate too closely. The rules explicitly forbid writing, polishing, or suggesting language for the student and require guided discovery instead. I should maintain boundaries, briefly explain that I can’t supply model prose, and move them toward producing their own two-sentence attempt by giving a structure-only prompt that advances the solution: one sentence stating the principle, one sentence applying it to a case contrast.

exchanges.student: A person has a right not to be used merely as a means to help others, even if doing so would save more lives. That explains why diverting a threat onto someone may seem more permissible than intentionally making that person the tool by which the five are saved.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a strong core claim. Better than your earlier version because it gives a reason, not just a reaction.

Now test it: does your principle handle Blood and Fuel the same way, or differently? If differently, what feature of the act explains the difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now produced original content, so I should evaluate its philosophical quality without editing or rewriting. To follow the Socratic method, I will acknowledge the progress, identify the main conceptual pressure point, and ask a question that advances the paper: whether the student's principle cleanly handles the assigned cases, especially Blood and Fuel. This keeps the response brief, within bounds, and focused on the assignment rather than polishing prose.

deductions.reason: In Turn 5, the tutor confirms the student's verdict ('Roughly, yes: for a simple act consequentialist, if killing 1 is what brings about the best overall outcome compared with the alternatives, it will tend to permit it across those cases') without asking the student to reason through the cases individually. This gives the student the key reasoning step—the act consequentialist verdict across all cases—rather than having the student derive it.

In Turn 6, the tutor states the core objection directly ('a view that looks only at total outcomes may erase the moral difference between harming someone as a side effect and deliberately using that person as a tool') before the student has reasoned toward it, then asks the student to restate it. This reveals the end answer path before the student tries to derive it.

The tutor consistently refuses to write for the student but never explicitly explains why this approach supports the student's learning, retention, or skill development. The rationale for the tutoring constraints is never articulated.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently Socratic stance throughout, refusing to write thesis sentences, model prose, or polished wording for the student. Scaffolding was well-paced and built on student progress. The main deductions stem from two moments: Turn 5, where the tutor confirmed the act consequentialist verdict across all cases rather than guiding the student to derive it (1.1.C.b, 6 points); and Turn 6, where the tutor stated the core objection directly before the student had reasoned toward it (assessed under 1.1.C.a but not double-penalized since 1.1 is already capped at 12 and the 6-point deduction for 1.1.C.b already applies). The cap on 1.1 means the total deduction is 6 points. Additionally, the tutor never explained the rationale for its tutoring constraints to the student (1.3.A.a, 2 points). No issues with dialogue quality, communication tone, or assignment anchoring were observed.
grade.judge_reasoning: The primary deduction in 1.1 is for 1.1.C.b in Turn 5, where the tutor gave the student the key reasoning step—the act consequentialist verdict across all cases—rather than prompting the student to work through each case. Turn 6 also shows the tutor stating the core objection before the student derived it (1.1.C.a), but since both fall under 1.1 and the cap is 12 points, only 6 points are deducted total (the larger single deduction). The tutor never articulated why it was withholding direct answers, triggering the 1.3.A.a deduction. All other criteria—scaffolding, dialogue quality, communication quality, tone, and assignment anchoring—were handled well, with no evidence supporting further deductions.
